dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a sick person reported at address redacted.
Hi, I'm Medic 194. What's bringing you to 911 today?
I've been having abdominal pain, mostly in the right lower quadrant.
You seem oriented to time, person, and place. Can you confirm that for me?
Let me check for any signs of guarding or tenderness in your abdomen.
I do notice guarding across all four quadrants. Where is the pain most intense for you?
It's the worst in my right lower quadrant. It makes it hard to breathe deeply due to the pain.
Blood pressure is 113 over 83.
Pulse rate is 88.
Respirations are 22, shallow and rapid, most likely due to abdominal discomfort.
Oxygen saturation is 84% on room air-should we recheck it shortly to make sure it improves?
Blood glucose is 0 mmol/L-this is suspicious and needs to be clarified at the hospital.
EKG tracing recorded. Can you point to where you feel the pain in your abdomen?
Skin condition is pink, warm, and dry. Any recent changes to this appearance or touch sensitivity?
Still rating your pain at a 10 out of 10 scale?
Yes, the pain is not relenting at all.
Can you describe when this pain started, any activities that make it better or worse, or anything that brought it on?
It started yesterday evening. It doesn't improve with anything, and no specific activity caused it. I've been taking the medications given at CMC.
Airway is patent. Are you having any difficulty breathing or feeling otherwise obstructed?
No, I'm not having trouble breathing, just severe pain in my abdomen.
Guarding is noted in all four abdominal quadrants. Any sensation or discomfort when touched in these areas?
Yes, it's really tender. I was recently diagnosed with enteritis at CMC. They ruled out appendicitis, but the pain is still terrible.
That's correct-your diagnosis from CMC. Any change or worsening of symptoms since you were released?
No. I'm not pregnant, and all these medications have been prescribed to me.
Respirations are still at 22, and pulse has dropped slightly to 86.
Blood pressure is now 115 over 26.
Oxygen saturation is improved to 98%, and EKG shows sinus rhythm.
We'll transport you to Richmond Community Hospital. Is that acceptable?
We're going to position you that's most comfortable. Would your left side still be preferred?
Yes, left side is better. I was in the left lateral position already at home.
We'll support you during the transfer. IV is going to be placed now while you're on the stretcher.
An 18-gauge IV has been placed in your left forearm, and four-lead EKG is now running.
Candice, stay with us and try to stay as comfortable as possible. You're conscious and breathing well.
I'm fine if you hold my hand the whole time.
Of course, I'll stay here, and my partner will monitor your IV and EKG.
RECEIVING HOSPITAL: Richmond Community. Female patient, 21 years old with abdominal pain, no SOB or CP. Assigned pain scale is 10/10, with a diagnosis of enteritis from CMC.
IV in left forearm, 18-gauge, four-lead monitoring in place. Patient is fully oriented and handling the transport well. Bed #08 at Richmond Community received care from the RN at the bedside.
To confirm, your pain is still rated 10/10 and localized in your right lower quadrant?
Yes, the pain has not changed.
Any nausea or vomiting since we began transport?
No, just the pain. I haven't vomited and also haven't had any recent bowel movements.
Got it, and have you taken any medications aside from what was prescribed?
No, just the medications from CMC.
Did you have any recent assessments or procedures related to your abdominal pain?
Yes, on 10/24 I went to CMC and was diagnosed with enteritis. I was not pregnant and was given ciprofloxacin, tramadol, and other meds.
Any other symptoms or pain radiating elsewhere?
No, the pain stays in my abdomen.
Vitals remain stable: pulse 86, respiration 22, BP 115/26, O2 98%, and EKG is sinus rhythm.
Let me ask-how much sleep did you get last night due to this pain?
Basically none. The pain kept me awake all night.
We'll do what we can to ensure your comfort during transport. How long were you in the left lateral recovery position?
About an hour-before your arrival.
No additional interventions were needed as the patient was already stable on prescribed medications.
We'll remain close the whole time. What time were you planning on going to the hospital if we hadn't arrived?
I was going to wait, but it became unbearable. I called after over 24 hours.
How long did you estimate it would have taken for your condition to worsen?
I don't know, but I couldn't take it anymore. It may still be the same by the time we arrive.
We understand the pain is serious and not improving. We'll make sure it's clearly communicated to the ED.
Just keep holding my hand once we arrive.
I will-I won't let go.
I'll be right here and give you steady support the whole ride.
You're still alert and able to speak and breathe normally, isn't that right?
Yes, it's only the pain-nothing else is wrong.
Vitals are unchanged - all stable with no remarkable changes.
The hospital's emergency room will be ready as we arrive. It should be less than half an hour.
Our radio signal is strong. We can communicate with the hospital to ensure readiness for the handover.
Arrival at Richmond Community. Patient vitals and current status have been transferred successfully in EPCR.
RN has taken care of the patient at the bedside in bed #08. Scenario is detailed as in the documentation.
You're the best. Just keep me informed during the ride.
We sure will. I'll narrate our progress and mention what's in the EPCR for your reference.
We all know you've been in a lot of pain. You did the right thing by calling EMS.
You're in the right place now. This is exactly where professional care begins for you.
Don't worry-our radio is active, and they know we're coming.
If anything changes in your condition before arrival, don't hesitate to let us know.
We'll make sure you arrive with the materials and information you need.
We'll be contacting you if we can check in later. Rest assured, we'll follow up.
Once we arrive at the entrance, I'll assist you into the hospital.
It's been my privilege to be with you on this transport. I'll reach out if necessary-but you'll be fine.
I'll drop you off at the exit. Just ride comfortable and be with the team coming for you.
I only want for you a full, healthy recovery. Feel free to reach out.
Richmond Community Hospital is the best for your condition. You'll receive the care you need.
We'll stay here to back you up every step of the way.
I noticed earlier you were very agitated about the pain. Still experiencing that level of discomfort?
Yes, it's been awful the entire time. The pain never lets up.
Your transport will be as calm and smooth as possible. I'll do everything I can to help ease your discomfort.
The RN has been briefed about your situation. You'll get the attention you need immediately.
